title: Black Holes
content: A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing—not even light—can escape its event horizon. The supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way, Sagittarius A*, has a mass roughly 4 million times that of the Sun.

title: Neutron Stars
content: Neutron stars are the collapsed cores of massive stars after supernova explosions. Incredibly dense, a teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tonnes. They can spin hundreds of times per second, emitting beams of radio waves as pulsars.

title: James Webb Space Telescope
content: Launched in December 2021, JWST is the most powerful space telescope ever built. Observing in infrared light, it can detect galaxies that formed just 300 million years after the Big Bang, peering back nearly 13.8 billion years.

title: Speed of Light
content: Light travels at approximately 299,792 km/s in a vacuum. Sunlight takes 8 minutes 20 seconds to reach Earth. Light from Proxima Centauri—our nearest stellar neighbor—takes 4.24 years to arrive.

title: The Milky Way
content: The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy hosting 100–400 billion stars. It spans about 100,000 light-years in diameter; our Solar System sits roughly 26,000 light-years from the galactic center and completes one orbit every 225 million years.

title: Mars
content: Mars has the tallest volcano in the Solar System—Olympus Mons at 22 km—and the longest canyon system, Valles Marineris, stretching 4,000 km. Its thin atmosphere and surface evidence of ancient riverbeds make it a prime candidate for past microbial life.

title: Voyager 1
content: Launched in 1977, Voyager 1 is humanity's most distant artifact, having entered interstellar space in 2012. It currently travels over 23 billion km from the Sun and still transmits data, with signals taking about 22 hours to reach Earth.

title: The Big Bang
content: The universe began approximately 13.8 billion years ago in an extremely hot, dense state. In the first fraction of a second it expanded exponentially. The cosmic microwave background radiation—a faint afterglow—is the strongest evidence for this event.

title: Dark Matter
content: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe but cannot be seen directly, as it does not interact with electromagnetic radiation. Its existence is inferred from gravitational effects on visible matter, such as the rotation curves of galaxies.

title: Exoplanets
content: Over 5,600 exoplanets have been confirmed orbiting stars beyond our Solar System. The TRAPPIST-1 system, 40 light-years away, contains seven Earth-sized rocky planets, three of which orbit in the habitable zone where liquid water could exist.

title: Solar Flares
content: Solar flares are bursts of radiation from the Sun's surface. The 1859 Carrington Event—the most powerful geomagnetic storm on record—disrupted telegraph networks worldwide. Modern equivalents could knock out satellites, power grids, and GPS systems.

title: Saturn's Rings
content: Saturn's iconic rings span up to 282,000 km in diameter but are remarkably thin—often less than 1 km deep. They consist mainly of ice particles and rocky debris ranging from tiny grains to chunks several metres across.

title: The International Space Station
content: The ISS orbits Earth at about 408 km altitude, traveling at 28,000 km/h and completing 16 orbits per day. Assembled from 1998 to 2011, it has hosted over 270 astronauts from 20 countries and is as long as an American football field.

title: Gravitational Waves
content: First directly detected in 2015 by LIGO, gravitational waves are ripples in spacetime caused by accelerating massive objects—such as merging black holes or neutron stars. They travel at the speed of light and confirmed a key prediction of Einstein's general relativity.

title: The Hubble Space Telescope
content: Launched in 1990, the Hubble Space Telescope has made over 1.5 million observations of more than 40,000 celestial objects. Its images helped pin down the age of the universe at 13.8 billion years and confirmed that the expansion of the universe is accelerating.

title: Ancient Egypt
content: Ancient Egyptian civilization flourished along the Nile for over 3,000 years. The Great Pyramid of Giza, built around 2560 BC for Pharaoh Khufu, stood as the world's tallest man-made structure for nearly 3,800 years. Hieroglyphic writing dates back to around 3200 BC.

title: The Roman Empire
content: At its peak under Emperor Trajan in 117 AD, the Roman Empire spanned 5 million km² and ruled over 70 million people—roughly 21% of the global population. Roman engineering achievements include aqueducts, roads, and the Colosseum.

title: Ancient Greece
content: Ancient Greece, particularly Athens in the 5th century BC, is considered the birthplace of democracy, philosophy, and the Olympic Games. Thinkers such as Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle shaped Western intellectual traditions for millennia.

title: The Library of Alexandria
content: Founded in the 3rd century BC in Egypt, the Library of Alexandria was the largest repository of knowledge in the ancient world, holding hundreds of thousands of scrolls. It attracted scholars from across the Mediterranean world.

title: The Silk Road
content: The Silk Road was a network of ancient trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean from around 130 BC. It facilitated not only the exchange of silk, spices, and gold but also the spread of religions, technologies, and diseases.

title: The Black Death
content: The Black Death (1347–1351) killed an estimated 30–60% of Europe's population—roughly 25 million people. Caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis and spread mainly by fleas on rats, it reshaped European society, economics, and religion.

title: The French Revolution
content: Lasting from 1789 to 1799, the French Revolution overthrew the monarchy, established a republic, and introduced Enlightenment ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity. It led to Napoleon Bonaparte's rise and reshaped European politics.

title: The Industrial Revolution
content: Beginning in Britain around 1760, the Industrial Revolution transformed economies from agrarian to manufacturing-based. Steam power, mechanized textile production, and railways enabled unprecedented economic growth and urbanization.

title: The Manhattan Project
content: A top-secret US research program (1942–1945), the Manhattan Project developed the first nuclear weapons. It employed over 130,000 people and culminated in the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945.

title: The Cold War
content: The Cold War (1947–1991) was a geopolitical struggle between the United States and the Soviet Union. It involved the nuclear arms race, the Space Race, proxy wars in Korea and Vietnam, and ultimately ended with the dissolution of the USSR in 1991.

title: Ancient China
content: China's history spans over 5,000 years. The Han Dynasty (206 BC–220 AD) established the Silk Road and saw advances in papermaking and the seismoscope. Chinese inventions including printing, gunpowder, the compass, and paper shaped the modern world.

title: World War I Origins
content: World War I (1914–1918) began after the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo. Underlying causes included a network of alliances, imperial rivalries, militarism, and nationalist tensions. It killed approximately 20 million people.

title: The Renaissance
content: The Renaissance (14th–17th centuries) was a cultural rebirth originating in Italy, marked by renewed interest in classical antiquity. Figures like Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, and Galileo made breakthroughs in art, architecture, and science.

title: The Age of Exploration
content: From the 15th century, European explorers—Columbus, Vasco da Gama, Magellan—mapped the Americas, Africa, and Asia. This era opened global trade routes and established European colonial empires, profoundly reshaping world demographics.

title: The Ottoman Empire
content: The Ottoman Empire lasted from 1299 to 1922 and at its height controlled territory across three continents—Southeast Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa. Constantinople (modern Istanbul) became its capital after 1453.

title: DNA Structure
content: DNA's double-helix structure was described by Watson, Crick, Franklin, and Wilkins in 1953. It encodes genetic information in sequences of four bases: adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine. The human genome contains approximately 3 billion base pairs.

title: Theory of Relativity
content: Einstein's special relativity (1905) showed that time and space are relative to the observer and that E=mc². General relativity (1915) described gravity as a curvature of spacetime caused by mass, predicting phenomena like black holes and gravitational lensing.

title: Quantum Mechanics
content: Developed in the early 20th century, quantum mechanics describes nature at atomic scales. Key principles include wave-particle duality, superposition, and the uncertainty principle. It underpins lasers, semiconductors, and MRI scanners.

title: Penicillin
content: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 when he noticed mold killing bacteria in a petri dish. Mass production during World War II saved millions of lives. Antibiotics remain among the most significant medical breakthroughs in history.

title: Human Genome Project
content: Completed in 2003, the Human Genome Project mapped all 3 billion base pairs of human DNA and identified roughly 20,000–25,000 genes. It cost about $2.7 billion; today the same sequencing costs less than $1,000.

title: The Internet
content: The internet evolved from ARPANET (1969), a US military network. Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN, creating the first browser and web server. Today over 5 billion people are connected.

title: The Transistor
content: Invented at Bell Labs in 1947 by Shockley, Bardeen, and Brattain, the transistor replaced bulky vacuum tubes. Modern microchips contain billions of transistors; the latest processors pack over 100 billion in a chip the size of a fingernail.

title: Artificial Intelligence History
content: AI as a field was formally founded at the 1956 Dartmouth Conference. Early milestones include chess programs (1950s), expert systems (1980s), and deep learning breakthroughs. In 2022–2023, large language models like GPT-4 brought conversational AI to mainstream use.

title: The Printing Press
content: Johannes Gutenberg's movable-type printing press (~1440) democratized knowledge by making books affordable. The Gutenberg Bible (1455) was among the first mass-produced books. The press accelerated the Renaissance and Protestant Reformation.

title: Vaccines
content: Edward Jenner developed the first vaccine in 1796 using cowpox to protect against smallpox. Vaccines have eradicated smallpox (1980), nearly eliminated polio, and prevented billions of cases of measles, diphtheria, and other diseases.

title: Periodic Table
content: Dmitri Mendeleev published the periodic table in 1869, arranging elements by atomic mass and noting periodic patterns in their properties. The modern table has 118 confirmed elements and successfully predicted the existence of elements yet to be discovered.

title: Evolution
content: Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, introducing natural selection as the mechanism of evolution. All life on Earth shares a common ancestor; DNA evidence confirms evolutionary relationships across species.

title: Plate Tectonics
content: The theory of plate tectonics, formalized in the 1960s, explains how Earth's lithosphere is divided into moving plates. Their interactions cause earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and the formation of mountain ranges over millions of years.

title: CRISPR Gene Editing
content: CRISPR-Cas9, developed into a practical gene-editing tool by Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier in 2012 (Nobel Prize 2020), allows precise editing of DNA sequences. It has potential applications in treating genetic diseases, cancer, and improving crops.

title: Climate Change
content: Human greenhouse gas emissions—primarily CO2 from burning fossil fuels—are raising global average temperatures. The IPCC reports that Earth has warmed by ~1.1°C since pre-industrial times. This drives more extreme weather, sea-level rise, and ecosystem disruption.

title: The Large Hadron Collider
content: The LHC at CERN near Geneva is the world's largest particle accelerator, with a 27 km circular tunnel. In 2012 it confirmed the existence of the Higgs boson—the 'God particle'—which gives other particles mass.

title: Quantum Computing
content: Quantum computers use qubits that can exist in superpositions of 0 and 1 simultaneously. This allows them to solve certain problems exponentially faster than classical computers. In 2019, Google claimed 'quantum supremacy' for a specific task.

title: Renewable Energy
content: Solar and wind energy are now the cheapest electricity sources in history. In 2023, renewables accounted for about 30% of global electricity generation. Solar panel costs have dropped by more than 90% since 2010.

title: Nanotechnology
content: Nanotechnology manipulates matter at scales of 1–100 nanometres—about the width of a DNA strand. Applications include targeted drug delivery, water filtration membranes, stronger and lighter materials, and nano-scale electronics.

title: Blockchain
content: A blockchain is a distributed, tamper-resistant ledger maintained by a decentralized network. Bitcoin (2009) was the first major application. Blockchain technology also underpins smart contracts, supply chain tracking, and digital identity systems.

title: Moore's Law
content: Proposed by Gordon Moore in 1965, Moore's Law observed that transistor density in integrated circuits doubles roughly every two years. This trend held for decades, driving exponential growth in computing power and reducing costs.

title: 3D Printing
content: Also called additive manufacturing, 3D printing builds objects layer by layer from digital designs. It is used in aerospace, medicine (custom prosthetics and bioprinting), architecture, and consumer goods. The first patent was granted in 1984.

title: The Human Brain
content: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others via synapses—creating roughly 100 trillion connections. It consumes about 20% of the body's energy while accounting for only 2% of its mass.

title: Photosynthesis
content: Photosynthesis converts sunlight, water, and CO2 into glucose and oxygen. It supports virtually all life on Earth by forming the base of the food chain. Land plants and ocean phytoplankton together produce about 120 billion tonnes of organic matter per year.

title: The Speed of Sound
content: Sound travels at approximately 343 m/s in air at 20°C, but this varies with medium and temperature. It moves about 4 times faster in water and 15 times faster in steel. A sonic boom occurs when an object exceeds this speed.

title: Amazon Rainforest
content: The Amazon Rainforest covers about 5.5 million km² across nine South American countries, primarily Brazil. It is the world's largest tropical rainforest, houses roughly 10% of all species on Earth, and produces about 20% of the world's oxygen.

title: Mount Everest
content: Mount Everest, on the Nepal–Tibet border, is Earth's highest peak at 8,848.86 m above sea level (2020 survey). It grows about 4 mm per year due to tectonic uplift. Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay first summited it on 29 May 1953.

title: The Great Barrier Reef
content: Located off Queensland, Australia, the Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, stretching 2,300 km. It hosts 1,500 species of fish, 4,000 types of mollusc, and 240 species of birds. It is visible from space.

title: The Mariana Trench
content: The Mariana Trench in the western Pacific Ocean reaches a maximum depth of about 11,034 m at Challenger Deep—deeper than Everest is tall. The water pressure there is over 1,000 times that at sea level. Only a handful of missions have reached the bottom.

title: The Sahara Desert
content: The Sahara is Earth's largest hot desert, covering about 9.2 million km² across North Africa. Surface temperatures can exceed 50°C, yet the Sahara was green and wet as recently as 5,000–11,000 years ago during the African Humid Period.

title: The Amazon River
content: The Amazon River carries about 20% of all fresh water that flows into the world's oceans. At its widest, it can be over 50 km across during flood season. It drains a basin of 7 million km² and is home to 2,500 species of freshwater fish.

title: The Nile River
content: The Nile, at about 6,650 km, is generally considered the world's longest river, flowing northward through northeastern Africa into the Mediterranean Sea. Its annual floods deposited fertile silt that sustained ancient Egyptian civilization for millennia.

title: Antarctica
content: Antarctica is Earth's fifth-largest continent and the coldest, driest, and windiest. It holds about 70% of the world's fresh water as ice. The lowest natural air temperature ever recorded on Earth, −89.2°C, was measured there in 1983.

title: The Northern Lights
content: The aurora borealis (Northern Lights) occurs when charged particles from the Sun interact with Earth's magnetic field and atmosphere, causing gases to glow. They are most visible in high-latitude regions like Norway, Iceland, Canada, and Alaska.

title: Volcanoes
content: There are roughly 1,500 potentially active volcanoes worldwide. The 1815 eruption of Mount Tambora in Indonesia is the largest recorded in history; it caused the 'Year Without a Summer' in 1816, leading to crop failures and famines across the Northern Hemisphere.

title: The Grand Canyon
content: Carved by the Colorado River over 5–6 million years, Arizona's Grand Canyon is 446 km long, up to 29 km wide, and over 1.8 km deep. Its exposed rock layers record nearly 2 billion years of Earth's geological history.

title: The Himalayas
content: The Himalayas are a mountain range in South Asia formed by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates, starting about 50 million years ago. The range contains 14 peaks over 8,000 m, including Everest, K2, and Kangchenjunga.

title: Coral Reefs
content: Coral reefs occupy less than 0.1% of the ocean floor but support about 25% of all marine species. They protect coastlines from storm damage and support fisheries that feed more than a billion people. Warming oceans cause coral bleaching, threatening their survival.

title: The Great Wall of China
content: The Great Wall of China stretches over 21,000 km when all sections are combined. Construction began in the 7th century BC and continued through the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644). Despite the myth, the Wall is generally not visible from space with the naked eye.

title: Stonehenge
content: Stonehenge on Salisbury Plain, England, was built in stages from about 3000 to 1500 BC. The iconic sarsen stones—each weighing up to 25 tonnes—were transported from Marlborough Downs, 25 km away. Its precise astronomical alignment suggests ritual purpose.

title: Machu Picchu
content: Built by the Inca Empire in the 15th century at 2,430 m altitude in the Peruvian Andes, Machu Picchu was abandoned during the Spanish conquest and unknown to the outside world until 1911. It is famous for its dry-stone walls that fit together without mortar.

title: The Pyramids of Giza
content: The Great Pyramid of Giza, built around 2560 BC, originally stood 146.5 m tall and contained an estimated 2.3 million stone blocks averaging 2.5 tonnes each. Ancient engineers aligned it with remarkable precision—within 0.05 degrees of true north.

title: Ocean Currents
content: Ocean currents are driven by wind, temperature, salinity differences, and Earth's rotation. The Gulf Stream transports warm water northward along the US East Coast and moderates Western European climates. Disruption of these currents could dramatically alter regional weather.

title: Earthquakes
content: Earthquakes occur where tectonic plates slip, releasing stored energy as seismic waves. The 1960 Valdivia earthquake in Chile, measuring 9.5 magnitude, is the strongest ever recorded. The Pacific Ring of Fire is the most seismically active region on Earth.

title: Biodiversity
content: Earth hosts an estimated 8.7 million species, of which only about 1.2 million have been scientifically described. Tropical rainforests contain the greatest biodiversity per km². Scientists estimate that current extinction rates are 100–1,000 times the natural background rate.

title: Niagara Falls
content: Niagara Falls, on the US–Canada border, is one of the most powerful waterfalls in North America, with a flow rate of about 2,400 m³ per second. The falls erode the rock beneath them by approximately 30 cm per year, slowly retreating upstream.

title: The Colosseum
content: The Colosseum in Rome, completed in 80 AD, could hold 50,000–80,000 spectators. It hosted gladiatorial contests, animal hunts, and mock naval battles for over 400 years. Its engineering innovations—including the arch and concrete vaulting—influenced architecture for centuries.

title: The Panama Canal
content: The Panama Canal, opened in 1914, cuts 77 km across the Isthmus of Panama, saving ships about 12,875 km compared to sailing around South America. Its lock system raises and lowers ships up to 26 m. About 14,000 vessels transit it annually.

title: The Water Cycle
content: The water cycle continuously moves water between oceans, atmosphere, and land through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and runoff. About 496,000 km³ of water evaporates from Earth's surface annually. The cycle regulates climate and distributes fresh water.

title: Human Space Exploration
content: Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on 12 April 1961. Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin first walked on the Moon on 20 July 1969. As of 2024, only 12 people have stood on the Moon. Artemis plans to return humans by the late 2020s.
